name: Waraqa ibn Nawfal
title: Waraqa ibn Nawfal (RA): The First to Confirm Prophethood
meta_description: The story of Waraqa ibn Nawfal (RA), the blind old scholar of Makkah who recognised the Prophet Muhammad after the first revelation and said the words: you are the prophet of this people.
keywords: Waraqa ibn Nawfal, story of Waraqa, first to confirm prophethood, cousin of Khadijah, Hanif of Makkah, sahaba stories
lede: He was a very old man, almost a century, and he had lost his sight. He had spent his whole life looking for one thing, and now it had walked through his door.

This is the story of Waraqa ibn Nawfal (may Allah be pleased with him): the lone Christian scholar of Makkah, a man who waited his entire life for a prophet, and the first to look at the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and confirm what had happened to him.

sections.h2: The seekers who walked away from the idols
paras: Long before Islam, a small number of people in Makkah refused the idols of their tribe. They were called the Hunafa, the seekers of the pure way of Ibrahim (peace be upon him). They wanted the worship of one God, and in a city built around its idols, they were willing to leave everything familiar to find it.

There were four of them. Two travelled north to Syria to study the older scriptures and deepen their knowledge of the Abrahamic way. One of those two was Zayd ibn Amr. The other was Waraqa ibn Nawfal. Of the four, three became Christian, and Waraqa was one of them. Only one of them, Zayd, did not, and he died some five years before the revelation came.

So by the time the moment arrived, Waraqa was the only one of those seekers still alive and still in Makkah. The others had died, or moved away. He remained, waiting, like a man who had kept a lamp burning for a guest who had not yet arrived. He had prepared himself for a prophet, and now there was no one else left in the city who had.

sections.h2: The noblest of a noble house
paras: Waraqa was the first cousin of Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (may Allah be pleased with her), though the gap in their ages was so large, perhaps twenty-five or thirty years, that he was more like an uncle to her. His family was among the most respected in Makkah, and Waraqa was counted the noblest of them.

By the measure of his people, he was the natural match for Khadijah: equal in lineage, equal in character. But he turned away from that life. He chose not to marry. He gave up the wealth of his rich tribe, dressed simply, ate only once a day, and gave his days to worship and his years to study and travel.

He was learned in a way few around him were. He read Hebrew, he wrote from the scriptures, and he was literate at a time when most were not. He was also not a man who fought his people. He kept to himself, away from the marketplace and the crowds, and trusted that the truth would be made clear in its own time.

Even the idol-worshippers of Makkah came to him to have their dreams interpreted. They cared little for their idols beyond the trade they brought, but they knew Waraqa was a man of real knowledge: a man who had studied language, scripture, and the ways of the older faiths. He was an elder whose word carried weight, and that was why, on the hardest day of her life, Khadijah knew exactly whose door to knock on.

sections.h2: A dream, and a quiet prediction
paras: When Khadijah was still a young woman, she had a dream that the sun descended into her home. She did not know what it meant, so she took it to Waraqa, the family elder who understood such things.

He told her it might mean that she would one day marry a man of great nobility, or even a prophet. Years later it was Waraqa who conducted her marriage to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He had no idea, when he spoke those words, how exactly they would come true.

Waraqa had always sensed that something was coming, and that it would come to this region. He was not the only one. There were signs around the Prophet ﷺ long before the cave, and they had been noticed by others on quiet roads far from home.

sections.h2: Signs on the road to Syria
paras: As a young man, the Prophet ﷺ travelled north with his uncle on a trade route to Syria. On the way, a monk who watched the caravan saw something and asked who was in charge of the boy. He warned the uncle that this child was no ordinary one, and told him to take him home and guard him, for his people might try to harm him if they knew who he was.

Later, when Khadijah sent the Prophet ﷺ to Syria with her trade, her servant Maysarah came back with stranger stories still. He said a cloud seemed to shade the Prophet ﷺ as he travelled, and that when he rested beneath a tree, the branches leaned to cover him. Another monk who saw him said only a prophet rests under that tree.

The people of Syria were used to dealing with traders by swearing oaths on their idols. The Prophet ﷺ would not. He told them he had never sworn by those idols in his life. Even before any revelation, those who looked closely kept seeing the same thing, and Waraqa, with his learning, was waiting for exactly this.

sections.h2: The day they came to his door
paras: After the first revelation in the cave of Hira, the Prophet ﷺ came home shaken. It was Khadijah who knew where to take him. She brought him to her old cousin, the man who had studied the scriptures and would understand what others could not.
Waraqa was in his nineties by then, and blind. He asked, gently, what the Prophet ﷺ had seen, expecting perhaps a dream to interpret. The Prophet ﷺ told him everything that had happened in the cave.

Waraqa did not hesitate. He said that the one who had come was the same angel God had sent to Musa (peace be upon him), the bearer of revelation. Then he turned to the Prophet ﷺ and said it plainly: you are the prophet of this people. After a lifetime of waiting, he knew.
The first thing Waraqa said next was not a question. It was a wish. "I wish I were young," he said. "I wish I could be alive on the day your people drive you out."

The Prophet ﷺ was startled. He had just been told he was a prophet, and now this old man was telling him his own people would turn on him. The Prophet ﷺ had never harmed anyone in Makkah. He was the peacemaker, the trusted one, the man tribes called on to settle their quarrels. He asked, will they really drive me out? Waraqa answered that no man had ever come with what he had come with except that his people took him as an enemy. It was never about the man, he said. It was about the message he carried.

Then this blind man of ninety made a promise. If I live to see that day, he said, I will support you with all of my strength, with everything Allah has given me. He did not ask Allah to make the path lighter. He asked for the strength to carry it. He died only a few days later, before the call to faith had even gone public.

ayah.text: Jesus, son of Mary, said, 'Children of Israel, I am sent to you by God, confirming the Torah that came before me and bringing good news of a messenger to follow me whose name will be Ahmad.' Yet when he came to them with clear signs, they said, 'This is obviously sorcery.'

paras: Scholars have long discussed how to count Waraqa. He believed before anyone else, and he died before the message became public, so some called him a believer who passed away in the pause before the call, and others wrote that he was indeed the first male companion. Either way, he was the first man on earth to confirm to the Prophet ﷺ that he was a prophet.

When Khadijah later worried about her cousin, who had believed and then died so soon, the Prophet ﷺ reassured her. He said he had seen Waraqa in a good state, dressed in white, and that had Waraqa been of the people of the Fire he would not have seen him so. In other narrations he saw him in the gardens of Paradise. Once, hearing a man speak ill of Waraqa, the Prophet ﷺ told him not to, and said he had seen Waraqa with not one garden but two.

There is a quiet beauty in where he sits in the story. The last time the Quran mentions Isa (peace be upon him) is when Isa foretells a messenger to come after him, whose name would be Ahmad. And the very first person to confirm that messenger had arrived was the last follower of Isa left in Makkah. One legacy was handed gently to the next.

lessons_intro: His whole part in the story is short: a few words, then a death a few days later. Yet a lifetime of quiet preparation stands behind those few words.

lessons.bold: Spend your life ready for the truth.
lessons.text: Waraqa did not stumble into that moment. He had searched, studied, and purified himself for decades, so that when the truth arrived he recognised it at once.

lessons.bold: Recognise the good in others without rivalry.
lessons.text: He was expected to be the great man of his family, yet his first instinct was to lift up another and to wish he could serve him.

lessons.bold: Ask for strength, not for an easier road.
lessons.text: He did not pray for a lighter burden. He prayed to be made strong enough to carry it. That is the difference between wishing well and meaning it.

lessons.bold: A sincere intention counts with Allah.
lessons.text: Waraqa died before he could lift a finger to help, yet his promise was true, and the Prophet ﷺ gave glad tidings of his place. Allah weighs what is in the heart.

faq.a: He was a cousin of Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (RA) and one of the few people of Makkah who rejected idols and sought the pure way of Ibrahim. A learned man who became Christian and read the older scriptures, he was the first to confirm the prophethood of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ after the first revelation.

faq.q: What did Waraqa say to the Prophet Muhammad?
faq.a: After hearing what happened in the cave of Hira, Waraqa said the one who came was the same angel sent to Musa, that the Prophet ﷺ was the prophet of his people, and that his people would one day drive him out. He promised that if he lived to see that day, he would support him with all his strength.

faq.q: Was Waraqa a companion of the Prophet?
faq.a: Scholars differ. He confirmed the Prophet's ﷺ message and believed in him, but he died only a few days later, before the call to Islam became public. Some count him as a believer who passed in that pause; others wrote that he was indeed the first male companion.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Waraqa?
faq.a: To prepare the heart for the truth so we recognise it when it comes, to honour the good in others without rivalry, to ask Allah for strength rather than an easier path, and to trust that a sincere intention is never wasted.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Waraqa spent almost a hundred years getting ready for a moment that lasted minutes. He never fasted a Ramadan, never prayed beside the Prophet ﷺ in a mosque, never saw the religion grow. His whole share was one true sentence and one sincere promise, offered at the end of a long life of seeking.

Most of us will not be asked for something dramatic either. We are asked, quietly, to keep our hearts honest and ready, so that when the truth is in front of us we do not miss it, and to mean the good we say we will do. It is easy to wait for a grand moment to prove our faith. Waraqa's life says the proof is in the long, ordinary years of seeking that come before it. His example is an invitation to live that way: prepared, glad for the good in others, asking only for the strength to carry what we are given. Carry one of those into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Waraqa ibn Nawfal (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 61:6). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was almost a hundred years old.

He had lost his sight.

He had spent his whole life looking for one thing.

And then it walked through his door.

Long before Islam, a few people in Makkah refused the idols and sought the pure way of Ibrahim.

Waraqa ibn Nawfal was one of them. He was the last one still in the city, still waiting.

He could have been the great man of his noble family.

Instead he chose not to marry, gave up his wealth, dressed simply, and gave his days to worship and study.

After the first revelation, the Prophet ﷺ came home shaken.

Khadijah knew where to take him: to her old, blind cousin who understood the scriptures.

Waraqa listened, then said it plainly:

"You are the prophet of this people."

He was the first man on earth to confirm it.

Then he made a wish no one expected.

"I wish I were young. I wish I could be alive the day your people drive you out, so I could stand with you."

He did not ask Allah for an easier road.

He asked for the strength to carry the burden. He died only a few days later, before the call had even gone public.

**A sincere intention counts with Allah.**

He never lifted a finger to help, yet his promise was true, and the Prophet ﷺ saw him in the gardens of Paradise.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
He was a very old man, almost a century, and he had gone blind. Waraqa ibn Nawfal (may Allah be pleased with him) had spent his whole life seeking the one true God, the last of the seekers still left in Makkah.

When the Prophet ﷺ came down from the cave of Hira afraid, it was Waraqa who recognised what had happened and said, "You are the prophet of this people." Then he wished he were young again, so he could stand beside him when the hard days came. He died only days later, before he could help at all. And still the Prophet ﷺ said he saw him in Paradise.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/waraqa-ibn-nawfal

email.subject: The blind old man who waited his whole life
email.alt_subject: "You are the prophet of this people"
email.preview: He spent almost a century seeking the truth, and recognised it the moment it walked through his door.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

The first man to confirm the prophethood of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was not one of his companions in the years to come. It was an old, blind man who died only days later.

His name was Waraqa ibn Nawfal (may Allah be pleased with him). Long before Islam, a small number of people in Makkah refused the idols and sought the pure way of Ibrahim. Waraqa was one of them, and by the time of the revelation he was the last of those seekers still living in the city.

He could have lived as the great man of his noble family. Instead he chose not to marry, gave up his wealth, dressed simply, ate once a day, and gave his years to worship and to studying the older scriptures. He read Hebrew. He was waiting, though he did not know exactly for what.

Then, after the first revelation, the Prophet ﷺ came home shaken, and Khadijah brought him to her old cousin. Waraqa listened to everything that had happened in the cave. He did not hesitate. He said the angel who came was the same one sent to Musa, and then he said it plainly: you are the prophet of this people.

What he said next is what stays with me. He did not celebrate. He wished he were young again, so he could be alive on the day the Prophet's ﷺ own people would turn on him and drive him out. And he promised that if he lived to see it, he would support him with all his strength. He did not ask for an easier road. He asked to be strong enough to carry it.

He died only a few days later, before he could lift a finger to help. And yet the Prophet ﷺ later said he had seen Waraqa in a good state, in the gardens of Paradise.

There is a quiet lesson in him. He spent a lifetime keeping his heart honest and ready, so that when the truth arrived he knew it at once. Most of us are asked for that, not for something dramatic: to stay ready, and to mean the good we promise.

May Allah be pleased with him, and gather us in the company of those who recognised the truth.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was a very old man, almost a century, and he had lost his sight. He had spent his whole life looking for one thing, and now it had walked through his door.

Waraqa ibn Nawfal (may Allah be pleased with him) was a cousin of Khadijah and the noblest man of a noble family. Long before Islam, he was one of a small group in Makkah who refused the idols and sought the pure way of Ibrahim. By the time of the revelation, he was the last of those seekers still living in the city.

He could have lived as a great man among his people. Instead he chose not to marry, gave away his wealth, dressed simply, and gave his days to worship and his years to studying the older scriptures. He read Hebrew, and he waited, though he did not know exactly for what.

Then the Prophet ﷺ came down from the cave of Hira, shaken, and Khadijah brought him to her old, blind cousin. Waraqa listened to everything that had happened. He did not hesitate. He said the angel who came was the same one sent to Musa, and then he said it plainly: you are the prophet of this people.

What he said next was a wish, not a question. "I wish I were young," he said, "so I could be alive the day your people drive you out, and stand beside you." He promised that if he lived to see it, he would support the Prophet ﷺ with all his strength. He did not ask for an easier road. He asked to be made strong enough to carry it.

He died only a few days later, before the call to faith had even gone public, before he could lift a finger to help. And yet the Prophet ﷺ later said he had seen Waraqa in a good state, in the gardens of Paradise.
There is a gentle beauty in where he stands. The last follower of Isa left in Makkah was the first to confirm that the promised messenger had come. One legacy was handed quietly to the next.
storybook.pullquote: You are the prophet of this people.
storybook.reflection: Waraqa spent almost a hundred years getting ready for a moment that lasted minutes, and he did not miss it. He asked Allah not for an easier path but for the strength to walk the hard one, and he meant the good he promised even though he never lived to do it. A heart kept honest and ready, and a sincere intention, are never wasted with Allah.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
